Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-14_19-17-31_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Failing test: "should create a new live cell at a dead position with exactly 3 live neighbors (reproduction)"
Expected behavior: For input [[0,0],[1,0],[0,1]], result should contain [1,1] (dead cell at (1,1) has exactly 3 live neighbors → becomes alive).
Current error: expected [] to deep equally contain [ 1, 1 ]
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-14_19-17-31_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts

Implement the minimal code to make this test pass while keeping the previous tests green. You can implement the full Game of Life logic now - it's reasonable since reproduction requires counting neighbors. A clean implementation: for each cell in input, increment a neighbor count for each of its 8 neighbor positions. Then any position with exactly 3 neighbors becomes alive, and any live cell with 2 neighbors stays alive (survival is also implicitly handled by counting). Or just handle reproduction (dead cells with 3 neighbors) for now if you want the minimal path.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text: I'll analyze the current state and implement the minimal code to make this test pass.
I'll implement a clean Game of Life implementation that handles reproduction and survival via neighbor counting.

content.text:
All 4 active tests pass (4 still todo).

Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Count neighbor contributions from each live cell into a Map. A position becomes alive if it has exactly 3 neighbors, or if it is currently alive and has exactly 2 neighbors.
**Result**: All tests now pass (4 passing, 4 todo)
**Approach**: This is the natural minimal implementation for reproduction since you must count neighbors to detect "exactly 3 live neighbors". The survival clause (`isAlive && count === 2`) is included because the counting approach naturally produces neighbor counts for live cells, and excluding it would require an extra filter step. All previously passing tests (empty input, underpopulation cases) continue to pass because cells with 0 or 1 neighbors don't meet either condition.
